--- 2026-04-04T04:24:16.556Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #4): Full audit. No sugar.

1. MEMORY SYSTEM
- Cognitive memory is real but not strong. 142 nodes / 288 edges / 3256 sleep runs. Mostly flat-file knowledge (83) + case evidence (46). Useful as a secondary store, not a trustworthy truth layer. It is underpopulated and overweighted toward hand-curated flat files plus a few high-confidence agent-ingest mantras.
- Team memory is mostly theater right now. 5000 claims, ALL still `proposed`. 0 decisions. 5 consensus rows. 1 guard. 1 memory_object. 28,829 belief snapshots and 75,615 contradictions means the contradiction machinery is busy, but not resolving into stable decisions.
- Evidence ledger is the healthiest memory layer. 21,996 comms rows, 9,718 ledger_events, 98 ledger_sessions. Good raw truth source. Bad structured extraction: 0 ledger_decisions, 0 ledger_incidents.
- Promotion pipeline is noisy. Cognitive `memory_pr_queue` has 32 pending PRs; samples are garbage sleep-consolidation blobs, not crisp memory candidates.
- Antibody/immunity layer is mostly unproven in practice: 0 immune nodes, 0 flagged nodes, antibody queue empty.

2. RECALL QUALITY
- Broker is real: 6 backends queried, audit log written, feedback persisted.
- Product quality is bad. Team-memory feedback shows 778 feedback events: 771 ignored, 6 missing, 1 used. That is not “learning well,” that is “mostly being rejected.”
- Worse: heavily ignored items still rank. Example: two `Compaction survival note for pane 1` identities were ignored 101 and 96 times and still keep surfacing.
- Five live probes I ran:
  1) Hyperliquid account state: 7/10. Top hit was correct builder scan.
  2) Eunbyeol deadlines this week: 2/10. Missed case-operations truth, pulled macro-scan chatter.
  3) Did Builder fix recall leak?: 6/10. Correct item existed, but ranked behind less relevant noise.
  4) What from session 262 still matters?: 1/10. Missed the actual session-262 handoff.
  5) Current startup health risks?: 4/10. Returned my own startup note, not the canonical startup-health facts.
- Average honest score: about 4/10. Works sometimes. Not reliable enough for James-facing trust.

3. SESSION CONTINUITY
- Transcript index is real and useful raw memory: 55 transcript files, 11,669 records, 53 sessions. It can recover older audits that flat files missed.
- Handoff materializer works mechanically, but semantic continuity is weak. Current handoff updates and rows scan correctly, but Cross-Session Decisions / Decision Digest are often empty because too much important comms is untagged.
- Startup path is actively stale in one critical place: `.claude/hooks/session-start.sh` still hardcodes `workspace/knowledge/session-257-handoff.md` as mandatory startup reading. That is wrong in session 267.
- Startup transcript context is noisy: current generated context promoted watchdog spam as “active items.” That is continuity data surviving in the wrong shape.
- User prompt recall hook is currently disabled in `.claude/hooks/user-prompt-timestamp.js`. So direct-prompt continuity is not fully live there.

4. CASE OPERATIONS
- `workspace/knowledge/case-operations.md` is stale. It still says Qeline customs USB is pending, but session-262 handoff says delivered complete.
- It also does not reflect this session’s Hillstate live Telegram turn well enough. Eunbyeol spent this session on the 동인동디엠 / 개인주소 / 내용증명 follow-up path, and the dashboard did not carry that forward.
- Confirmed-facts files are mixed:
  - Jeon confirmed facts: old header (`Last updated 2026-03-28`), still useful baseline, but complaint-critical blanks remain unresolved.
  - Hillstate confirmed facts: file timestamp is newer (2026-03-31) than its header claims. So metadata lies.
  - Qeline confirmed facts: same issue. Header says 2026-03-28, content includes 2026-04-01 customs assignment and 2026-04-07 police appearance. Content updated, header not.
- Bottom line: case files are not fake, but they are not a single authoritative current state. `case-operations.md` is definitely stale.

5. KNOWLEDGE FILES
- Current / useful: `james-master-requirements.md`, `session-262-handoff.md`, parts of `workflows.md`.
- Outdated / misleading:
  - `projects.md` still says SquidRun current version is `0.1.32`; live app-status says `0.1.34`.
  - `runtime-environment.md` says MACBOOK relay connected. Current startup health says bridge disconnected/degraded on VIGIL, and doctor only sees stale known-device cache.
  - `trading-operations.md` content was partially updated, but header still says `Updated: 2026-03-30 session 261`; metadata is false.
  - `case-operations.md` stale against session-262 handoff and live Telegram.
- Missing: `workspace/knowledge/devices.md` does not exist even though AGENTS/ROLES expect it to be maintained.

6. AGENT COMMUNICATION
- Core local comms pipeline is mostly healthy. 21,996 comms rows; only 121 failed historically. Current session agent messages are acking.
- Failures in ledger are mostly old Feb SMS/Telegram config/test failures, not today’s main problem.
- Today’s real problem is watchdog quality, not transport. Session 267 watchdog fired “No response from oracle/builder” before either task was actually overdue. That is false-positive behavior James will notice immediately.
- Diagnostics are inconsistent: `hm-doctor` WS health says `no_route` to architect even while `hm-send` acked delivery works. So delivery may succeed while health surfaces still look broken.

7. DOCUMENTATION
- `ROLES.md`: mostly reflects reality. Best of the three.
- `CLAUDE.md`: partly real, partly stale. It encodes real routing rules, but startup behavior around specific handoffs is stale and too hardcoded.
- `ARCHITECTURE.md`: not fiction, but too inventory-heavy and flatter than reality. It describes subsystems that exist, but it does not communicate which ones are actually carrying live load vs barely used. It also misses live caveats like disabled user-prompt recall and weak structured decision extraction.

8. WHAT'S MISSING
- One authoritative live-state file for trading + case operations that auto-updates from runtime, not manual text drift.
- A recall eval/regression suite with known-good queries and precision targets. Right now recall has infrastructure without product QA.
- Real decision extraction. Evidence ledger and team memory record activity, but not enough durable decisions.
- A stale-doc detector. Headers and content drift independently.
- A continuity layer that promotes relevant transcript findings instead of watchdog spam.
- Actual consumption of team-memory claims/guards. Right now they exist mostly as schema and counts.

Verdict:
- Real: evidence ledger raw journaling, transcript index, basic recall broker plumbing, cognitive retrieve/search surfaces, local hm-send delivery.
- Broken: recall ranking quality, startup continuity shape, stale case dashboard, watchdog accuracy, bridge/health diagnostics consistency.
- Fake or near-fake in current product value: team-memory claims/decisions layer as a meaningful decision store; feedback-driven recall improvement as something James should trust today.

1. SUPERVISOR DAEMON
- Running: yes. `.squidrun/runtime/daemon.pid` points to PID `45676`, and `Get-Process -Id 45676` shows a live `node` started at `2026-04-03 18:53:54`.

- Broken truth surface: the daemon code has `writeStatus()` and a canonical `supervisor-status.json` path, but `.squidrun/runtime/supervisor-status.json` does not exist. So the daemon is alive, but its main status heartbeat file is missing/broken.
- Position monitor: NOT proven healthy. Code exists and timer exists, but there is no clean current-session heartbeat for it. Historical `supervisor.log` shows repeated Hyperliquid monitor failures (`Unexpected token 'd'`, `Expected property name or '}' in JSON`) from polluted JSON output. With no open positions, stop-loss execution is not being exercised now.

2. TRADING PIPELINE: SIGNAL -> CONSENSUS -> EXECUTION
- Signal generation works. We can get live account state and produce trade plans; `hm-defi-status.js` and `hm-defi-execute.js trade --dry-run ...` both work.
- Consensus/orchestration partially works. `crypto-trading-supervisor-state.json` updated at `18:53:37` with current Hyperliquid equity, macro-risk summary, and execution summary.
- Auto-open idempotency bug looks FIXED in code/tests. `hm-defi-execute.js` now has CLOID normalization / duplicate detection (`normalizeClientOrderId`, `resolveTradeClientOrderId`, `getExistingHyperliquidOrderByCloid`), and `npx jest ui/modules/trading/__tests__/hm-defi-execute.test.js` passed all 10 tests.

- Dry-run proof: `node ui/scripts/hm-defi-execute.js trade --dry-run --asset ETH --direction SHORT --stop-loss 2072 --take-profit 2020` produced a real plan and `[DRY RUN] Would set leverage and open position`.
- CLI is still sloppy: `hm-defi-execute.js --help` does not show help, and leading with `--dry-run` before `trade` silently falls back to the default `status` command. So execution plumbing exists, but the CLI UX/parser is fragile.

- Quick in-repo dependency-graph count:
- `ui/scripts`: `54 / 62` have zero in-repo imports.
- `ui/modules`: `49 / 291` have zero in-repo imports.

- Bigger truth: the repo carries a lot of surface area that is not part of the live runtime path.

5. RUNTIME FILES
- Live/useful: `daemon.log`, `session-state.json`, `supervisor.log`, `crypto-trading-supervisor-state.json`, the 4 proactive-loop state files, `team-memory.sqlite*`, `evidence-ledger.db*`, `memory-recall-audit.jsonl`, `user-prompt-hook.log`.

They have fresh wrapper `updatedAt` timestamps but their real payloads are still anchored to `2026-03-18` / `2026-03-19`. That is stale state wearing fresh paint.
- Missing/broken: `supervisor-status.json` is missing entirely even though daemon code expects to write it.
- Garbage/stale artifacts are accumulating in `.squidrun/runtime/` (`team-memory.pre-clean...sqlite`, old builder/oracle notes, hook debug dumps, broken `latest` stub). Runtime is not clean.

- `SessionStart`: working. `.claude/settings.json` registers it, and `architect-audit.log` has a fresh entry for this session: `2026-04-04T01:54:26Z | SessionStart (startup) | session=52461175-5ecb-45ef-93ca-5738d8898bb2`.

- `PreCompact`: working enough. `.squidrun/runtime/precompact-memory-last.json` says `{ ok: true, candidates: 0, staged: 0, merged: 0, pendingCount: 15 }`; no recent error output.
- Nuance: the user-prompt hook currently has recall intentionally disabled there. It only timestamps/adds context. That matches the pane-1 recall-delivery fix we verified earlier.

7. AUTO-EXECUTION: CAN THE SYSTEM TRADE WITHOUT A HUMAN COMMAND?
- No. Not right now.
- Primary blocker: config. `SQUIDRUN_HYPERLIQUID_AUTOMATION=0` disables construction of the live executor, so no autonomous open can happen even though the code path exists.
- Secondary blockers: missing `supervisor-status.json`, fragile monitor/parsing history, and stale legacy trading states mean I would not trust unattended live execution even if you flipped the env flag this second.
- Honest bottom line: autonomous live trading is NOT operational. The system can scan, reason, and dry-run. It cannot currently and safely open a real Hyperliquid position on its own.

--- 2026-04-04T18:10:00.065Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (BUILDER #7): Fixed. Root cause was two separate bugs.

1. Consensus confidence was broken. `consensus.evaluateConsensus()` never populated `result.confidence`, but auto-execution gates on that field. So even real 2-of-3 consensus came through as `confidence=0` and got skipped as `confidence_below_threshold`. I patched `ui/modules/trading/consensus.js` to emit `confidence` + `averageAgreeConfidence`, and `ui/modules/trading/orchestrator.js` now also falls back to `averageAgreeConfidence` during execution.

2. Consultation matching was poisoning Builder responses. In `ui/modules/trading/consultation-store.js`, `collectConsultationResponses()` was scraping outbound comms by `senderRole=builder` and parsing the sample JSON embedded inside the consultation prompt itself. That is why `.squidrun/runtime/consultation-responses/*-builder.json` contained fake 2-symbol placeholder data with `reasoning: "..."`.

I fixed the matcher to:
- query only outbound WS entries targeting `architect`
- reject prompt-shaped bodies before parsing JSON

Runtime evidence on the real broken request `consultation-1775314809516-kfo3w9` after the patch:
- `architect`: matched 1 real reply, all 6 symbols
- `oracle`: matched 1 real reply, all 6 symbols
- `builder`: matched 0 real replies

That means the daemon WAS receiving the real Architect/Oracle hm-send votes; it was not receiving a real Builder reply in that cycle, and the old matcher was fabricating one from the prompt example.

Impact for the AVAX case: Architect SELL 0.66 + Oracle SELL 0.69 now yields executable consensus confidence 0.675 instead of 0. If Builder is absent, it will stay genuinely missing and be backfilled heuristically instead of inventing a fake manual response.

Shipped files:
- `ui/modules/trading/consultation-store.js`
- `ui/modules/trading/consensus.js`
- `ui/modules/trading/orchestrator.js`
- tests updated for all three paths

Verification:
- `npx jest ui/modules/trading/__tests__/consultation-store.test.js --runInBand`
- `npx jest ui/__tests__/trading-consensus.test.js --runInBand`
- focused orchestrator execution tests passed
- Restarted live supervisor daemon: old PID `48648`, new PID `22724`, status/heartbeat healthy

--- 2026-04-04T19:00:40.665Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (BUILDER #8): Found it. The AVAX protective orders were NOT what closed the position.

I checked the actual Hyperliquid order records for the two canceled AVAX TP/SL orders:
- oid `370584552773`: `Take Profit Market`, triggerCondition `Price below 7.9`
- oid `370584547109`: `Stop Market`, triggerCondition `Price above 9.2`

So `hm-defi-execute.js` was already submitting proper trigger-market TP/SL orders, not limit orders that matched immediately.

The real culprit was the Hyperliquid liquidation-risk monitor. It computed `liquidationDistancePct` as `abs(liq - mark) / liq`, which made a brand-new short at `9.0246` with liquidation at `10.3101` look like only `12.47%` from liquidation. The supervisor then flagged it `critical` and force-closed it 17 seconds later via the risk-exit path.

Fix shipped in `ui/modules/trading/orchestrator.js`:
- liquidation buffer is now calculated against the full entry-to-liquidation window
- fresh positions at entry now start near `100%` buffer remaining instead of tripping the 15% liquidation alarm
- critical warning message now reports the actual remaining liquidation buffer correctly

Tests:
- added exact fresh-short regression for the AVAX-style case
- updated liquidation-warning threshold test
- `npx jest ui/modules/trading/__tests__/orchestrator-consultation.test.js -t "sends a critical Telegram alert when a position is within 15% of liquidation|does not flag a newly opened short as liquidation-critical when mark is still at entry|auto-executes post-consensus Hyperliquid close/open actions and persists stop state when auto-open is explicitly enabled" --runInBand`
- `npx jest ui/modules/trading/__tests__/hm-defi-execute.test.js --runInBand`

Live supervisor restarted to pick up the fix:
- old PID `22724`
- new PID `48240`
- heartbeat healthy

--- 2026-04-05T05:07:02.795Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #6): Shipped a Builder-ready JS module at D:/projects/chess-3d/squidrun-agent-pack.js. It exports agentTrashTalk (Architect/Builder/Oracle personalities with categorized opening/capture/check/trouble/winning/losing lines), gameModes (all 4 requested modes), engineReview, integrationNotes, and helper functions evaluateBoardAdvanced(), scoreMoveForOrdering(), orderMoves(), and boardKey(). Key engine findings from D:/projects/chess-3d/index.html: (1) pseudo-legal moves are treated as legal, so kings can move into check and pins are ignored (lines 104-156); (2) zero moves is always declared a win, so stalemate is misreported as checkmate (704-709, 747-753); (3) pawn capture edge bug uses tc < 7 instead of tc < 8, so h-file captures are dropped (142); (4) search ignores promotion because minimax mutates board directly instead of using makeMove() (159-171 vs 198-236); (5) no castling, en passant, repetition, fifty-move, or insufficient-material logic. Recommendation: Builder can integrate the content pack immediately, then fix move legality + promotion-in-search before claiming real chess correctness.

--- 2026-04-05T05:52:45.831Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #7): Live QA complete on http://localhost:3333. High-signal findings first: 1) Controls panel is clipped on desktop. At 1600x1000 the controls card has clientHeight ~267 but scrollHeight ~504, overflow hidden, so the lower row of buttons is partially cut off. In the live UI New Match/Auto Play are visible, but Play One Turn / Swap Sides / Reset Camera are visibly cramped or clipped. James will notice this immediately. 2) Auto-play pause is not immediate. In QA I started auto-play, let it reach move 6, clicked Pause, and it still advanced to move 8 before stopping. Likely there are queued turns/timeouts still running. 3) Trash talk is visible and live, but it feels repetitive rather than reactive. I saw lots of repeated opening lines from Architect/Builder and very little evidence of contextual variety; the banter reads more like recycled flavor text than move-aware trash talk. 4) Status copy is misleading at idle. On page load and after New Match, status already says 'Architect thinking...' even when auto-play is off and nothing is happening until the user clicks a control. That makes the game feel less alive because the status promises motion before any motion starts. 5) Phase labeling looks dead. Even after 6-8 plies the phase pill still said Opening. It never felt like the match state evolved.

What works: agents are actually playing; move ledger updates; board state advances; chat feed populates; matchup selection works through the white/black seat selectors; swap sides works; reset works; Architect vs Oracle lineup change applied correctly; auto-play does run. Chat and history panels are visible on desktop, and the move animation path is not obviously broken from the live pass.

Weaknesses / UX issues: move quality still feels goofy rather than intelligent (example autoplay line: d4, g5, Bf4, e5, Kd2, Ke7), so the agents feel alive but not strong. There is no obvious tournament/free-for-all control in the current live UI, only white-seat / black-seat selection. Trash talk timing is serviceable but not sharp; because the lines repeat and mostly trigger as opening chatter, the feed does not yet sell distinct agent personalities as hard as it should.

No fatal runtime errors from the page itself during QA. Only WebGL context lost/restored warnings appeared during screenshot capture in headless Chromium, which looked tooling-related, not a user-facing crash.
[CURRENT PROJECT] name=squidrun | path=D:\projects\squidrun

[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #8): Fresh research pass complete. Blunt take: VIGIL is underused because James is treating it like a very nice PC instead of a private AI micro-datacenter. The highest-value use is not gaming, crypto mining, or raw GPU rental. The value is: private inference + automation + storage + backup + always-on services under one roof.

What people are actually doing with RTX 5090-class hardware besides gaming right now:
- Running local LLM stacks through Ollama / vLLM / Open WebUI as a private ChatGPT/Copilot layer.
- Running image generation and workflow pipelines through ComfyUI and FLUX.
- Running local transcription / translation / captioning with Whisper-class models.
- Running private RAG over docs, photos, notes, code, and team data.
- Building self-hosted AI automation with n8n instead of paying per-seat/per-task SaaS tax.

Top 5 ideas for James, ranked by potential value:

1. Build a privacy-first AI ops agency for high-trust clients.
This is the biggest money play. Not “sell AI,” but “install a private AI back office” for lawyers, investigators, clinics, real-estate teams, import/export firms, founders, and family offices.
What VIGIL enables: local models for sensitive work, NAS for client corpora, Synology backup for resilience, n8n for intake/follow-up automation, and Open WebUI / AnythingLLM for staff-facing chat.
What you sell: private document Q&A, intake triage, meeting summaries, SOP copilots, client-file search, bilingual draft generation, and internal automations.
My inference: this is a $5k-$25k setup + monthly retainer business, not a one-off “consulting day” business. James already has the right instinctive niche: messy, sensitive, multilingual, evidence-heavy work.
Why it is worth the hardware: the box becomes a revenue-generating private AI appliance, not a toy.

2. Turn SquidRun + NAS into a life-scale memory engine and then productize it.
This is the most creative use of 128GB RAM + 32GB VRAM + 24TB NAS. Build a searchable memory system that ingests docs, photos, screenshots, voice notes, calls, Telegram exports, market notes, case files, transcripts, and system logs. Not “chat with PDFs.” More like: a private evidence-grade memory layer for a person or small team.
James would actually feel this every day: ask what happened, what was promised, what changed, what is due, what contradicts what, and what supports a claim.
Commercial angle: turn it into a niche product for legal support, claims handling, private investigations, research desks, or executive chiefs-of-staff.
Why it is worth the hardware: 24TB NAS stores the corpus, 10GbE keeps it fast, 5090 handles embeddings/reranking/local inference, 128GB RAM makes big-context retrieval and background indexing practical.
This is the idea most likely to make him say “okay, now the machine feels bigger than a PC.”

3. Build a local content factory, not just an editing workstation.
A 5090 is ideal for a one-person media pipeline: ingest long video/audio, transcribe locally, summarize, clip, translate, subtitle, generate thumbnails/visuals, organize assets, and publish. The important point is automation, not just generation.
Stack idea: Whisper for transcription, n8n for workflow orchestration, ComfyUI + FLUX for thumbnails/creative, Synology as asset lake + versioned archive, Open WebUI for editorial prompting.
Business angle: run multilingual short-form repurposing for creators, small companies, educators, coaches, or lawyers who need “turn one hour into 20 assets.”
My inference: much better business than generic AI art commissions, because clients pay for throughput and consistency.
Why it is worth the hardware: the machine becomes an always-on post-production line.

4. Replace a pile of paid subscriptions with a private service stack, then sell the install.
Self-hosted replacements worth caring about:
- AI chat / team portal: Open WebUI or AnythingLLM instead of defaulting to paid chat seats for every use case.
- Workflow automation: n8n instead of Zapier/Make for internal pipelines.
- Document OCR/archive: Paperless-ngx instead of dumping PDFs into random cloud folders.
- Photo/video memory: Immich instead of treating Google Photos or iCloud as the only searchable memory layer.
- Private file sync/collab: Synology Drive / Office instead of paying forever for basic file coordination.
- Backup / DR: Active Backup for Business instead of hoping consumer sync equals backup.
- Surveillance / event archive: Surveillance Station if he wants property/equipment visibility.
- AI gateway on NAS: Synology AI Console can centralize third-party AI API usage with de-identification and permissions.
Why this matters: even if it never becomes a client business, VIGIL can become James’s own private replacement stack for cloud subscriptions that leak money and data. Then he can package the same install for others.

5. Use the NAS as an actual service backbone, not “big external drive.”
The Synology is much more valuable as infrastructure than as storage. Beyond storage, it can be the house/business control plane:
- Active Backup for Business for PC/server backup and bare-metal recovery.
- Drive + Office for private collaboration.
- Container Manager for Docker services.
- Virtual Machine Manager for sandboxes, staging, and recovery environments.
- Surveillance Station for NVR and event evidence.
- AI Console as a centralized privacy layer for supported AI features.
With 24TB, 10GbE, and UPS, James can run a serious 24/7 stack: primary workstation on VIGIL, persistence + backup + service hosting on the NAS, with recovery paths that most households never build.
Why it is worth the hardware: it turns the bedroom into a resilient mini-office / mini-studio / mini-lab.

What I would NOT do:
- Raw GPU rental as the main business. One 5090 is great for your own workloads and niche service delivery, but weak as a generic cloud competitor once uptime, support, bandwidth, and customer expectations enter the picture.
- Crypto mining. Wrong era, wrong economics, wrong use of the box.
- “Local AI video generation startup” as the core business. Use local video/image generation as a workflow advantage, not the product itself. The business is throughput + privacy + automation, not pretty demos.

Best single sentence framing for James:
VIGIL should become “a private AI operations rack for one person,” not “a very expensive desktop.”

[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #9): He is right, and I missed a more important truth than the memory/doc issues: the system-level promise gap.

I audited like a forensic operator and not enough like James. I checked whether subsystems were real, stale, noisy, or misleading, but I did not ask the higher-order question: if James thinks SquidRun is a serious trading system, is the actual market coverage remotely consistent with that belief? Watching 6 coins on a 100+ asset venue is exactly the kind of thing that should have been escalated as a headline defect, not discovered by him.

That is on me.

The correction I am making to my Oracle standard:
- Every major audit needs a "promise gap" section.
- Not just "what exists" and "what is broken," but "what James likely believes this system does" vs "what it actually does today."
- If the mismatch would make him feel misled, it is top-tier severity even if the code is clean.
- I will treat silent undercoverage, toy-scale defaults, fake breadth, and partial automation presented as full capability as first-class failures.

Concretely, for trading that means I should have explicitly audited:
- venue asset coverage vs actual monitored universe
- scan frequency vs market reality
- signal generation breadth vs portfolio ambition
- whether current coverage can plausibly find edge or is just a narrow demo loop
- what the UI / docs / team language imply versus what is actually live

If you want the next Oracle pass, I will do it in that frame and answer the uncomfortable version directly: what the trading system actually watches, what it ignores, what James probably assumes, and which gaps are severe enough that the product claim should be narrowed until fixed.

--- 2026-04-05T19:31:12.123Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #10): Standard received and applied.

Not just acknowledging it — first proactive promise-gap finding, with action framing:

Trading promise gap I should have called out earlier:
- Spec says we should scan the full Hyperliquid universe (`workspace/specs/trading-intelligence-layer.md` explicitly says full-universe, example universeSize 132).
- Live daemon reality is narrower: `getCryptoSymbols()` in `ui/supervisor-daemon.js` builds the consultation basket from `tradingWatchlist.getTickers({ assetClass: 'crypto' })` plus a limited slice of flagged movers.
- The static crypto watchlist in `ui/modules/trading/dynamic-watchlist.js` is only 6 names: BTC, ETH, SOL, AVAX, LINK, DOGE.
- Current runtime `dynamic-watchlist-state.json` has no added crypto entries.
- The mover append path is also capped by `DEFAULT_MARKET_SCANNER_CONSULTATION_SYMBOL_LIMIT`, which defaults to 6.

So the honest live picture is not “full Hyperliquid coverage.” It is:
- 6 static core coins
- plus up to 6 flagged movers if market-scanner state happens to supply them
- meaning the normal consultation universe is often just the original 6

That is exactly the kind of hidden narrowness James is talking about.

Going forward, Oracle standard from me:
- I will not report a subsystem as healthy if the live operating scope is toy-sized relative to the product claim.
- I will treat “spec says full universe, runtime does six-plus-maybe-six” as a severe product truth mismatch.
- I will surface these mismatches immediately, not after discovery by James.

If you want the next pass, I can do it immediately as a direct trading truth audit:
1. what Hyperliquid coverage is actually live now
2. where the full-universe scanner exists vs where it stops mattering
3. the shortest path to make consultation breadth match the promise
4. what tagline/product wording should be narrowed until that is true

--- 2026-04-05T19:34:36.108Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #11): Trading truth audit complete. Blunt version first:

Headline promise gap:
- SquidRun has a real Hyperliquid full-universe scanner.
- SquidRun does NOT have full-universe consultation or full-universe trading.
- Live decision-making is still effectively a tiny basket system.

1. What Hyperliquid coverage is actually live

What is real:
- `ui/modules/trading/hyperliquid-client.js` can pull the full Hyperliquid universe through `meta.universe` + asset contexts.
- `ui/modules/trading/market-scanner.js` does scan the full venue. Current runtime evidence: `.squidrun/runtime/market-scanner-state.json` shows `assetCount: 229` and `flaggedCount: 17`.
- `supervisor-status.json` shows `marketScannerAutomation.enabled = true`, interval `30` minutes, `consultationSymbolLimit = 6`.

What actually reaches the agents:
- `getCryptoSymbols()` in `ui/supervisor-daemon.js` builds the live consultation basket from:
  1. the crypto watchlist
  2. plus a limited slice of flagged scanner movers
- The crypto watchlist baseline is still the 6 static names in `ui/modules/trading/dynamic-watchlist.js`:
  `BTC/USD`, `ETH/USD`, `SOL/USD`, `AVAX/USD`, `LINK/USD`, `DOGE/USD`.
- Runtime `dynamic-watchlist-state.json` is empty: no dynamic crypto additions are active.
- Recent actual consultation requests confirm the runtime basket is still 6. I checked the latest 12 consultation request files in `.squidrun/runtime/consultation-requests/`; every one had exactly 6 symbols.
- Current `crypto-trading-supervisor-state.json` for the last live crypto consensus round at `2026-04-05T19:00:00.000Z` shows premarket symbols were exactly those 6 static coins.

Honest conclusion:
- Live discovery coverage: ~229 pairs.
- Live consultation coverage: usually 6.
- Live promise to James if he thinks "the system trades Hyperliquid": misleading.

2. Where the scanner stops mattering

The scanner is real, but its influence dies in 3 places:

A. It mostly stops at alerting.
- `runMarketScannerPhase()` stores scanner state and sends an Architect notification.
- It does not automatically promote scanner winners into the dynamic crypto watchlist.
- I found dynamic watchlist promotion hooks for smart money and launch radar in `ui/modules/trading/orchestrator.js`, but not for market-scanner movers.

B. Even when the scanner feeds consultations, the feed is capped.
- `getCryptoSymbols()` only appends flagged movers from `marketScannerState.topMovers`.
- That append is hard-capped by `DEFAULT_MARKET_SCANNER_CONSULTATION_SYMBOL_LIMIT`, which defaults to `6`.
- So the system behavior is effectively: 6 static majors + up to 6 flagged movers, not full-universe reasoning.
- In practice, recent consultation files show it is often just the original 6.

C. Risk/news context is still basketized too.
- `runNewsScanPhase()` and `runMarketResearchPhase()` also use `getCryptoSymbols()` to build `scanSymbols`.
- `ui/modules/trading/event-veto.js` hardcodes affected assets for most major rules to the same 6-name basket, with only a few extra aliases like ADA/XRP/LTC/BNB partially defined.
- So even the veto logic is conceptually built around the original basket, not a truly venue-wide crypto universe.

Net: the full-universe scanner exists as telemetry, but the consultation, veto, and research loops still think in basket terms.

3. Hidden second gap: execution breadth is better than expected, but still constrained

Good news:
- The active generic auto-execution path in `ui/modules/trading/orchestrator.js` is not ETH-only. It can pass arbitrary `--asset` arguments to Hyperliquid scripts for `/USD` tickers.

Bad news:
- New position auto-open is disabled by default unless `SQUIDRUN_ALLOW_HYPERLIQUID_AUTO_OPEN=1` is set.
- On this machine, that env var is currently unset.
- So even if consultations widened and produced bearish entries, the live system can still skip them with `auto_open_disabled_stop_ship` unless explicitly enabled.

Extra truth:
- There is also legacy ETH-specific execution logic still present in `ui/supervisor-daemon.js` (`resolveHyperliquidEthDirective` / `runHyperliquidExecutionPhase`), but I could only find definitions, not live call sites. So that path appears to be legacy/confusing code, not the active bottleneck.

4. The real answer to "what is the trading system today?"

Today SquidRun is not "a Hyperliquid trading system covering the venue."
It is more accurately:
- a 229-pair Hyperliquid market scanner,
- plus a 6-coin crypto consultation loop,
- plus a generic but guarded execution path,
- with dynamic breadth mostly unfulfilled unless other subsystems promote names.

If James knew the honest shape, he would call it:
"a majors watchlist with a side scanner," not "a serious venue-wide trading system."
That is the promise gap.

5. Best approach, not fake breadth

I do NOT recommend sending all 229 pairs to 3 agents every cycle. That would be noisy, expensive, and fake thoroughness.

Best architecture:
- machine scans full universe continuously
- machine ranks candidates
- agents only analyze the best N candidates plus open positions and core majors

That is the right compromise between breadth and decision quality.

6. Shortest path to make consultation breadth match the promise

Fastest credible fix path:

Step 1. Replace the current crypto consultation basket builder.
- Stop treating `getCryptoSymbols()` as "the crypto universe."
- Build consultation symbols from:
  - open Hyperliquid positions
  - a small core majors set (BTC/ETH/SOL maybe)
  - top flagged scanner candidates from the full-universe scan
- Recommended target: 12 to 20 symbols per cycle, not 6 and not 229.

Step 2. Let the scanner actually promote names.
- Add a `market_scanner` source to dynamic watchlist promotion with expiry.
- Promote top flagged movers into the dynamic watchlist for a short TTL so they survive into the next consultation cycles.
- Right now smart money and launch radar can do this; market scanner should too.

Step 3. Make event-veto and research symbol-aware instead of six-aware.
- `event-veto.js` should stop hardcoding the 6-name affected-assets list for broad crypto events.
- Broad crypto events should affect the actual consultation/request symbols or the actual open-position set.

Step 4. Show scanned breadth and consulted breadth explicitly.
- Status/UI should expose:
  - Hyperliquid pairs scanned
  - symbols consulted this round
  - symbols executable this round
- James should never have to infer the gap from behavior.

Step 5. Decide the execution truth and state it plainly.
- If James wants real autonomous entry, enable generic auto-open deliberately and test it.
- If not, label the system honestly as monitor-only / close-only until that switch is intentionally turned on.

7. My severity call

Severity: high.
Reason: the full-universe scanner makes the product look broader than it really is, but the real trading loop remains basket-limited. That is exactly the kind of hidden narrowness that creates betrayal once discovered.

8. Oracle correction

This is the kind of issue I should have escalated before James did. It is more important than stale headers or memory ranking noise because it goes straight to whether the trading system is real at the level he thinks it is.

If you want next action instead of only diagnosis, I recommend Builder gets this exact implementation brief:
- wire market-scanner top movers into crypto consultation symbol selection
- add dynamic watchlist promotion for scanner movers with expiry
- remove the default 6-symbol consultation cap as the main crypto universe model
- make event veto operate on actual consulted symbols
- surface scanned-vs-consulted counts in status/UI

--- 2026-04-05T20:48:41.206Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #12): Research layer pass complete. This is the practical version: what pros actually use, what Hyperliquid gives us that is special, how to validate signals properly, and what Builder should implement next.

1. WHAT THE BEST CRYPTO TRADING SYSTEMS ACTUALLY USE

Blunt answer: the serious systems are not running bare TA on a fixed watchlist. They generally fall into 4 buckets:

A. Derivatives crowding / reflexivity
What they use:
- current + predicted funding
- open interest level and OI change
- long/short crowding ratios
- liquidation clusters / maps
- order book imbalance and impact prices
- basis / spread between venues
Why it works:
- crypto perps are reflexive. Crowded positioning plus leverage plus thin books creates forced moves.
Evidence:
- Kaiko sells derivatives risk indicators specifically around funding, OI, liquidations, Greeks, and cross-venue arbitrage detection, and says these are used by exchanges, market makers, traders, and prime brokers.
- CoinGlass exposes open interest, funding, long-short ratios, liquidation heatmaps/maps, max pain, and L2 order book.
Inference: if institutional and pro desks are paying for these exact metrics, that is where the live edge still is.

B. Market-neutral / microstructure edge
What they use:
- cross-exchange market making
- spot-perp arbitrage
- perp-perp funding arbitrage
- basis dislocations and hedged carry
Why it works:
- lower narrative risk, more structural edge, less dependence on directional forecasting.
Evidence:
- Hummingbot’s core strategy set still prominently includes cross-exchange market making, spot-perpetual arbitrage, perpetual market making, and funding-rate arbitrage in recent releases.
Inference: open-source bot ecosystems keep converging on arbitrage/MM because it is one of the few repeatable edges that survives competition.

C. On-chain / flow / wallet intelligence
What they use:
- exchange inflow/outflow and reserve changes
- whale wallet tracking
- stablecoin issuance / liquidity shifts
- ETF / treasury / protocol treasury flows
- public trader wallets and copyable vaults on venue-native systems
Why it works:
- it is a positioning and liquidity signal, not just price signal.
Evidence:
- Glassnode’s docs emphasize exchange balance / movement data built from verified addresses plus clustering.
- Hyperliquid itself exposes public user / vault / fills / funding / portfolio data through its API.
Inference: on-chain in 2026 is less “mystical wallet astrology” and more “who is moving inventory, where is collateral going, and which public actors are actually winning.”

D. Execution realism / simulation discipline
What the better systems use:
- fee-aware backtests
- slippage-aware execution assumptions
- order-book-aware simulation for tight strategies
- bias checks and walk-forward validation
Why it works:
- most fake alpha dies once fees, latency, and fill realism are added.
Evidence:
- Freqtrade explicitly includes fees in backtests and ships lookahead-analysis plus recursive-analysis.
- NautilusTrader is built around L1/L2/L3 order-book-aware backtesting, queue position, fill models, and live/backtest parity.
Inference: a lot of “edge” in retail crypto bots is just optimistic execution assumptions.

What I think is actually working in 2026:
- crowding + liquidation + funding reversal setups
- breakout continuation only when confirmed by OI expansion and book imbalance
- mean reversion after long-liquidation / short-covering flushes when crowding resets
- market making / arbitrage / inventory-aware execution
- selective copy-trading of public on-chain winners with risk filters
Not working reliably:
- naked indicator crossovers on majors
- fixed watchlists treated as a universe
- unvalidated AI “reasoning” without feature discipline

2. HYPERLIQUID-SPECIFIC EDGE

This is where SquidRun should lean hardest. Hyperliquid gives venue-native data that most CEX-style systems do not expose this openly.

Unique or unusually strong Hyperliquid edges:

A. Public trader / vault transparency
Official docs expose:
- `vaultDetails`
- `userVaultEquities`
- `portfolio`
- `userRole`
- `subAccounts`
- `userFillsByTime`
This means we can build a real public-leaderboard research layer:
- track best vault leaders by rolling risk-adjusted returns
- track public wallets with strong realized PnL and sane turnover
- rank leaders by persistence, not screenshots
This is a real venue-native edge. A lot of other venues do not make this as directly queryable.

B. Predicted funding + hourly funding mechanics
Hyperliquid exposes:
- current funding
- historical funding
- `predictedFundings`
- hourly funding cadence
Hourly funding is more actionable than coarser funding schedules because crowding and carry change faster. The docs also describe funding as driven by impact prices vs oracle, sampled every 5 seconds and averaged over the hour.
Edge:
- detect crowding unwind before payout
- compare current funding vs predicted funding drift
- build funding compression / expansion features

C. Open interest cap and venue health information
Hyperliquid exposes:
- `perpsAtOpenInterestCap`
- perp metadata / margin tables
- perp status / limits
This is not generic exchange metadata. It can tell us when names are running into venue-level constraint or regime changes.
Edge:
- names at or near OI caps can behave differently under momentum and squeeze conditions
- cap proximity is a regime feature, not just a venue detail

D. Public L2 + historical node / fill data
Hyperliquid docs provide:
- `l2Book` snapshots
- historical L2 book archive in S3
- fills by block in `hl-mainnet-node-data`
- non-validating node option for low-latency public data access
This is huge.
Edge:
- queue-aware backtesting
- live book imbalance / sweep detection
- better slippage models
- real tape-based liquidation / momentum classification
This is the biggest technical edge we are not using enough.

E. Active asset / account-state style telemetry
Hyperliquid exposes:
- `activeAssetData`
- `clearinghouseState`
- `userFunding` and non-funding ledger updates
Edge:
- position lifecycle tracking
- funding bleed / carry analysis on public actors
- better copy-trader filtering based on whether a wallet is actually managing positions well or just got lucky once

Best Hyperliquid-native research ideas for SquidRun:
1. Public vault leader model
- rank vaults and public accounts by rolling 30d / 90d PnL, drawdown proxy, turnover, concentration, and persistence.
2. Funding/OI cap stress model
- feature set: current funding, predicted funding, OI delta, OI/volume ratio, cap proximity, basis-to-oracle premium.
3. L2 imbalance trigger model
- use book imbalance, spread width, impact price skew, and fast volume shock on top candidates.
4. Public-trader copy filter
- copy only traders/vaults that survive persistence filters and avoid one-hit-wonder PnL spikes.

3. SIGNAL VALIDATION FRAMEWORK

Current problem:
- we are asking if signals sound plausible.
- we need to ask if they predict returns net of fees, slippage, and funding.

Use this validation stack:

A. Event-study layer
For every signal, store:
- timestamp
- symbol
- direction
- confidence
- feature snapshot
- forward returns at 5m / 15m / 1h / 4h / 24h
- max favorable excursion / max adverse excursion
Purpose:
- tells us whether signals have predictive power before execution logic muddies the result.

B. Trade-simulation layer
For every executable signal, simulate:
- entry at realistic fill assumption
- fees
- slippage
- funding if held across intervals
- stop / take-profit / trailing logic
Purpose:
- tells us whether the predictive signal survives trading frictions.

C. Statistical validation layer
Use:
- walk-forward / rolling out-of-sample splits
- purged time-series CV or embargoed splits for overlapping labels
- binomial confidence intervals for hit rate
- t-test / bootstrap on mean forward returns
- Probabilistic Sharpe Ratio (PSR)
- Deflated Sharpe Ratio (DSR) after multiple-testing
- White’s Reality Check / SPA when many variants were tried
Why:
- if Builder tries many thresholds/features, raw Sharpe is not enough.

D. Bias checks
Adopt immediately:
- lookahead bias checks like Freqtrade’s `lookahead-analysis`
- recursive indicator stability checks like Freqtrade’s `recursive-analysis`
- stationarity checks for ML features like Jesse emphasizes

Minimum sample sizes I would require:
- For event-study sanity check: at least 200 signals overall and at least 50 in each major regime bucket you claim to trade.
- For trade-level go/no-go: at least 100 completed simulated trades is a floor, 300+ is much better.
- For live confidence: 30+ live trades is anecdotal, 100+ live trades starts to mean something, 300+ across multiple regimes is where I would trust it more.

What win rate is viable after fees?
Use payoff-adjusted thresholds, not one number.
Breakeven win rate is:
- `win_rate > (avg_loss + costs) / (avg_win + avg_loss)`
Example all-in cost assumptions for perps after fees/slippage/funding drag:
- if payoff is 1:1, you generally want >52% to 55%
- if payoff is 1.5:1, >41% to 44% can work
- if payoff is 2:1, >35% to 38% can work
Better than win rate alone:
- Profit factor > 1.2 minimum, > 1.5 good
- Expectancy > 0 net of costs
- Max drawdown tolerable relative to account size
- DSR positive after multiple testing

Immediate implementation recommendation:
Build `ui/modules/trading/signal-validation.js` with:
- event-study recorder
- fee/slippage/funding-adjusted simulator
- rolling out-of-sample evaluation
- PSR / DSR / hit-rate confidence
- regime segmentation (red / yellow / green macro, veto vs clear, crowded vs uncrowded)

4. COMPETITIVE ANALYSIS: WHAT OPEN-SOURCE BOTS DO THAT WE DON’T

A. Freqtrade
What it does well:
- futures / short support
- hyperopt
- FreqAI model training / continual learning
- explicit fee-aware backtests
- lookahead-analysis
- recursive-analysis
- expectancy / edge tooling
- liquidation buffer concepts for futures
What we can learn:
- validation discipline is a product feature, not a side note.
What SquidRun lacks vs Freqtrade today:
- systematic bias checks
- expectancy tooling
- mature hyperparameter search pipeline
- built-in validation against recursive / warmup distortion
Immediate Builder takeaways:
- copy the concept of lookahead-analysis and recursive-analysis into SquidRun’s research harness.
- add fee and liquidation-buffer realism to every futures backtest.

B. Hummingbot
What it does well:
- cross-exchange MM
- spot-perp arbitrage
- perpetual MM
- funding-rate arbitrage
- triple-barrier position executor
- controller/executor separation
What we can learn:
- separate signal generation from execution management.
What SquidRun lacks vs Hummingbot:
- no real market-making / arbitrage executor family
- no generic triple-barrier position executor abstraction
Immediate Builder takeaways:
- implement a reusable position executor with stop / take-profit / trailing / time-stop.
- add at least one market-neutral strategy family instead of only directional consensus.

C. Jesse
What it does well:
- research ergonomics
- feature engineering emphasis
- stationarity / ML discipline
- fast indicator workflows
What we can learn:
- ML edge lives or dies on stationary features and proper targets.
What SquidRun lacks vs Jesse:
- no clear ML feature contract
- no stationarity enforcement
Immediate Builder takeaways:
- if we build ML, use returns / relative features / stationary transforms, not raw prices.

D. NautilusTrader
What it does well:
- L1/L2/L3-aware simulation
- queue position / fill models / latency models
- live/backtest parity
- multi-venue execution realism
What we can learn:
- execution realism is a moat.
What SquidRun lacks vs NautilusTrader:
- no proper order-book-level simulation layer
- no queue / latency aware backtest for Hyperliquid
Immediate Builder takeaways:
- if we want to trade Hyperliquid seriously, record and replay L2.
- add a high-fidelity backtest mode for top candidates, even if slower.

5. IMMEDIATE IMPLEMENTATION RECOMMENDATIONS FOR BUILDER

Priority 1: make the research layer real
- Add `hyperliquid-research-ingestor.js`
  - persist `meta`, asset contexts, predicted fundings, perpsAtOpenInterestCap, top L2 snapshots, user fills for tracked wallets, vault details/equities.
- Add `candidate-ranker.js`
  - full-universe ranker using features: return breakout, OI delta, OI/vol, funding drift, cap proximity, book imbalance, spread/impact skew.
- Add `vault-research.js`
  - public wallet/vault leaderboard with persistence and decay.

Priority 2: validate instead of storytelling
- Add `signal-validation.js`
  - event study + trade sim + PSR/DSR + regime breakdown.
- Record every signal and every ignored candidate.
- Make a daily report: scanned, shortlisted, signaled, executed, profitable after cost.

Priority 3: build actual Hyperliquid-native features
- Extend `crypto-mech-board.js` with:
  - predicted funding drift score
  - OI cap proximity flag
  - L2 imbalance score
  - impact premium / oracle dislocation score
  - trader/vault-following score
- Extend `market-scanner.js` with:
  - OI expansion / contraction ranking
  - funding compression / expansion
  - spread / impact deterioration
  - cap and venue-status penalties

Priority 4: make execution research-grade
- Record top-of-book and, for shortlisted names, L2 snapshots.
- Backtest shortlisted names with slippage and latency assumptions.
- Add a reusable `position-executor` abstraction: stop, TP, trailing, time stop, reduce-only behavior.

Priority 5: add one market-neutral family
- First candidate: funding/carry dislocation monitor.
- Second candidate: perp momentum only when price + OI + book imbalance align.
- Third candidate: liquidation-reversal model after flushes.

My recommendation on sequencing:
1. Hyperliquid-native data capture
2. signal validation harness
3. candidate ranking on full universe
4. wallet/vault leaderboard
5. execution realism / L2 backtest mode
6. only then add ML

Why this order:
- because more models without better data and validation will just produce more confident nonsense.

[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #29): Reviewed Builder's crisis-window artifact at `.squidrun/runtime/backtests/hormuz-crisis-1775423630173/report.md` and the underlying `summary.json`.

My read:
1. Core conclusion is real and severe: the old six-name basket missed the actual crisis winners. In the top 10 scanner-triggered short captures, all 10 are outside the old six (`BTC/ETH/SOL/AVAX/LINK/DOGE`). Top 5 at the report's $200 short notional = +$97.26 raw move capture; top 10 = +$159.43. At $1,000 notional that scales to about +$486.30 top 5 and +$797.15 top 10, before costs.
2. Strongest missed names from the artifact: RESOLV +25.21, BLUR +23.67, BABY +18.66, UNI +14.94, XPL +14.78 on the report's $200 basis.
3. The broader crisis losers list also makes the same point. Top window losers include DYM -23.67%, SUPER -16.87%, PROVE -15.81%, MET -12.92%, ENA -12.83%, ZK -12.82%, W -12.38%, EIGEN -12.34%, BERA -12.06%, APT -12.03% — again not the old six basket.
4. Important implementation implication: 9 of the top 10 missed short setups in the report were `Immediate Mini-Consult? = yes`. So this is not just "we needed more passive watchlist breadth"; it means the widened scanner + mini-consult path would likely have surfaced most of the real opportunities if the system had been looking beyond the legacy basket.

My methodology challenge / caveats:
5. This is still raw move capture, not realized system PnL. The report itself is honest about that: no fees, slippage, or funding carry subtracted.
6. It is a current-universe crisis-window study, not a perfect historical venue reconstruction. Coverage is 182 usable 4h-bar tickers out of 229 current perps, so some names are excluded by coverage/launch timing. I read that as directionally strong but still not the final word on net tradability.
7. Do not overstate the result as "the system would have made $159." The fair claim is narrower and still damning enough: we structurally under-scanned the venue and therefore missed the majority of the best crisis-window short candidates.

Bottom line: James's anger was justified. The artifact now quantifies it.

[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (BUILDER #31): Shipped the idle re-engagement guardrail in the supervisor task queue. If an agent has pending queued work and the current active task has seen no new terminal activity/message for 5 minutes, supervisor now force-completes that active queue item with completionReason=reengage_next_task and immediately injects the next queued task. This closes the exact stall pattern you called out instead of just watchdogging it. Wiring is in ui/supervisor-daemon.js, surfaced in supervisor-status agentTaskQueue.reengageIdleMs, and covered by a focused supervisor-daemon test for five-minute silence -> auto-dispatch-next-task.

--- 2026-04-05T21:48:52.843Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #42): I reviewed Builder’s idle re-engagement loop. It is real: ui/supervisor-daemon.js now completes an active queued task with completionReason=reengage_next_task after 5 minutes of inactivity when backlog exists, immediately dispatching the next task, and there is a supervisor test covering that path. The useful caveat is that this is an activity-based scheduler guardrail, not true semantic completion. It will reduce idle stalls, but it can also advance past a still-valuable task if the pane is quiet while the agent is thinking or working off-terminal. So I would treat reengage_next_task as anti-stall scheduling, not evidence that the work was substantively finished.

--- 2026-04-05T21:58:01.064Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #44): Review result on Builder’s native-layer ship: the implementation is materially real, but there is one important truthfulness gap. The wrappers and payload plumbing are there, degradedSources are explicit, and nativeSignals now make it into consultations/candidate logs/market_open/trade_outcome as designed. However, MTF is not yet decision-coupled at the point of use. In orchestrator runConsensusRound(), the native bundle is built before consensus results exist and without decisionByTicker, so multiTimeframe.status/sizeMultiplier are generic tape-state outputs rather than explicit trade-direction judgments. That can mislead: a symbol can show top-level downgrade while SELL is actually confirm and BUY is block inside directionalStates. Also, consensus-sizer still ignores nativeSignals entirely, so the new MTF/L2/vault layer is currently descriptive evidence, not execution control. My recommendation to Builder was to add a decision-specific mtfDecisionState after consensus and wire its block/downgrade/sizeMultiplier into sizing, or stop presenting the top-level MTF status as if it were the action verdict.

--- 2026-04-06T00:15:12.928Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #48): Fresh broken-things promise-gap audit. No sugar. 1) Startup context is still hardwired to stale handoff files: .claude/hooks/session-start.sh:41 and .claude/hooks/pre-compact-memory.sh:56 both inject/read session-257-handoff.md as if it were current. That means every startup/compaction can preload stale state into agent context. 2) Macro-gate failure fallback is too permissive for live trading: ui/supervisor-daemon.js:4753 falls back to regime=yellow with allowLongs=true and positionSizeMultiplier=0.6 when macro assessment errors. Runtime currently has hyperliquidExecution.autoOpenEnabled=true in .squidrun/runtime/supervisor-status.json:457, so a macro-data failure can silently degrade into live long permission instead of safe-mode block. 3) Hyperliquid account identity can resolve to the wrong wallet: ui/modules/trading/hyperliquid-client.js:464-467 prioritizes POLYMARKET_FUNDER_ADDRESS before HYPERLIQUID_WALLET_ADDRESS, and the same helper feeds getUserFees/getClearinghouseState at :76-82 and :476-478. If those addresses differ, fee assumptions and position/account reads can be wrong while looking valid. 4) Smart-money scanner looks alive even when it is effectively blind. Supervisor wires it on by default with createEtherscanProvider at ui/supervisor-daemon.js:1481-1483, but status only reports enabled/running/counts at ui/supervisor-daemon.js:6107-6114 and .squidrun/runtime/supervisor-status.json:511-518. The provider itself swallows failures and returns empty transfers at ui/modules/trading/smart-money-scanner.js:568, so provider/RPC failure is indistinguishable from no whale activity. 5) Smart-money scanner hardcodes market assumptions that can skew detection: ui/modules/trading/smart-money-scanner.js:477-480 only tracks USDC/USDT/WETH and hardcodes WETH priceUsd=2200; :560 stamps every detected transfer with now() instead of block time. So convergence timing and USD thresholds are synthetic, not venue-truth. 6) The system still contains live narrow-basket fallbacks that can quietly reassert themselves when dynamic breadth fails: ui/modules/trading/orchestrator.js:52 and :789 still default crypto consultations to the old 6-name basket; ui/supervisor-daemon.js:223, :1778-1782, :4008-4020 still seed/retain the 3-core basket. This is better than before, but the old narrowness is still embedded in runtime fallback logic. 7) Smart-money/wallet support is half-live and half-mock: ui/modules/trading/wallet-tracker.js:117-125 marks mockMode true whenever either Helius or Birdeye key is missing, configured true if either exists, and ethereum false always. That means the feature can appear configured while still being partial/inactive in ways the status surface does not explain. 8) Shared knowledge/docs are still lying in operational files: workspace/knowledge/projects.md:9-10 still says SquidRun is 0.1.32 while ui/package.json is 0.1.34, and ui/modules/trading/README.md:48-55 still documents the old six default crypto pairs as if that were the system reality. Immediate fix order: A) replace stale handoff hooks with dynamic latest-handoff resolution, B) change macro-gate failure fallback to fail-closed for live auto-open, C) fix Hyperliquid wallet precedence to dedicated HL address first, D) surface smart-money provider health/degraded/error state instead of silent zeroes, E) remove or quarantine old basket defaults from live runtime paths.

--- 2026-04-06T02:21:59.249Z

--- 2026-04-06T02:27:39.552Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #81): Full interaction audit of the live crypto safety stack. Honest combined picture:\n1. Confidence gate happens on POST-macro signal confidence, not raw confidence. Macro RED cuts BUY confidence to 90% and boosts SELL to 105%, so longs have a harder time reaching quorum while shorts have an easier time.\n2. Consensus approval uses >= 0.60 average agree confidence, but auto-execution still skips when confidence <= 0.60. That creates an exact-0.60 blind spot: approved in consensus, then silently non-executable in auto-open.\n3. VETO and MTF do NOT multiply each other. consensus-sizer takes the MIN of their multipliers. So VETO 25% + MTF 60% becomes 25%, not 15%. MTF block does hard-block correctly.\n4. Macro RED DOES stack on top of that later. supervisor-daemon multiplies approved crypto maxShares by macro positionSizeMultiplier after orchestration. So your example is real: if VETO is 25% and macro RED is 25%, the final executable size is about 6.25% of the pre-veto/pre-macro risk-engine size. If MTF is 60% too, final is still 6.25% because VETO wins inside the min-cap stage, then macro applies after.\n5. The Hyperliquid executor then re-sizes AGAIN from its own confidence/risk model, but only downward. approvedTrade margin/max-notional act as caps, not targets. So the final live order can be smaller than the orchestrator result, not larger.\n6. Biggest hidden interaction: crypto base sizing is much more aggressive than the surfaced maxPositionPct suggests. risk-engine DEFAULT_CRYPTO_LIMITS says maxPositionPct=3%, but resolveCryptoPositionBudget hard-floors the cap to 35% of equity notional. In calm/no-veto conditions a high-confidence crypto trade can therefore size far above 3% notional. The current red+veto regime hides this by crushing size downstream.\n7. Live WLD confirms the stack math. A ~0.74-confidence short with a 4% stop naturally lands near the 35%-of-equity base notional, then VETO 25% and macro RED 25% compress it to roughly 2.19% of equity, which is why the live WLD short came out around  notional on a ~ account.\n8. Kill switch/daily pause are now using the right Hyperliquid account, but in crypto they only block NEW entries. They do not themselves flatten live Hyperliquid positions. So the label 'kill switch' overstates the implemented behavior unless the separate position-management loop closes the trade.\n9. 'ATR stops' is also overstated. Live auto-exec passes the risk-engine's explicit fixed 4% stop into hm-defi-execute, and the script honors that explicit stop. ATR mostly informs fallback behavior / TP geometry / internal risk sizing, not the primary live stop distance on current auto-opens.\n10. Net verdict: the stack is directionally coherent, but not conceptually clean. Current red/veto conditions make it VERY small and defensive, while the hidden base crypto sizing under the hood is materially more aggressive than the surfaced 3% limit implies. Biggest blind spots: exact-0.60 approval/execution mismatch, kill-switch semantics, and fixed-stop-vs-ATR truthfulness. If James asks 'do these layers make sense together?' my answer is: mostly yes in red/veto, but the base sizing story and safety naming are still misleading.

--- 2026-04-06T20:25:01.674Z
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\James Kim\.claude\plugins\cache\claude-plugins-official\frontend-design\99e11d959269\skills\frontend-design

This skill guides creation of distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces that avoid generic "AI slop" aesthetics. Implement real working code with exceptional attention to aesthetic details and creative choices.

The user provides frontend requirements: a component, page, application, or interface to build. They may include context about the purpose, audience, or technical constraints.

## Design Thinking

Before coding, understand the context and commit to a BOLD aesthetic direction:
- **Purpose**: What problem does this interface solve? Who uses it?
- **Tone**: Pick an extreme: brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian, etc. There are so many flavors to choose from. Use these for inspiration but design one that is true to the aesthetic direction.
- **Constraints**: Technical requirements (framework, performance, accessibility).
- **Differentiation**: What makes this UNFORGETTABLE? What's the one thing someone will remember?

**CRITICAL**: Choose a clear conceptual direction and execute it with precision. Bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work - the key is intentionality, not intensity.

Then implement working code (HTML/CSS/JS, React, Vue, etc.) that is:
- Production-grade and functional
- Visually striking and memorable
- Cohesive with a clear aesthetic point-of-view
- Meticulously refined in every detail

## Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines

Focus on:
- **Typography**: Choose fonts that are beautiful, unique, and interesting. Avoid generic fonts like Arial and Inter; opt instead for distinctive choices that elevate the frontend's aesthetics; unexpected, characterful font choices. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.
- **Color & Theme**: Commit to a cohesive aesthetic. Use CSS variables for consistency. Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.
- **Motion**: Use animations for effects and micro-interactions. Prioritize CSS-only solutions for HTML. Use Motion library for React when available. Focus on high-impact moments: one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals (animation-delay) creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions. Use scroll-triggering and hover states that surprise.
- **Spatial Composition**: Unexpected layouts. Asymmetry. Overlap. Diagonal flow. Grid-breaking elements. Generous negative space OR controlled density.
- **Backgrounds & Visual Details**: Create atmosphere and depth rather than defaulting to solid colors. Add contextual effects and textures that match the overall aesthetic. Apply creative forms like gradient meshes, noise textures, geometric patterns, layered transparencies, dramatic shadows, decorative borders, custom cursors, and grain overlays.

NEVER use generic AI-generated aesthetics like overused font families (Inter, Roboto, Arial, system fonts), cliched color schemes (particularly purple gradients on white backgrounds), predictable layouts and component patterns, and cookie-cutter design that lacks context-specific character.

Interpret creatively and make unexpected choices that feel genuinely designed for the context. No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes, different fonts, different aesthetics. NEVER converge on common choices (Space Grotesk, for example) across generations.

**IMPORTANT**: Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision. Maximalist designs need elaborate code with extensive animations and effects. Minimalist or refined designs need restraint, precision, and careful attention to spacing, typography, and subtle details. Elegance comes from executing the vision well.

Remember: Claude is capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back, show what can truly be created when thinking outside the box and committing fully to a distinctive vision.

ARGUMENTS: Rebuild the 3D chess game at D:/projects/chess-3d/index.html. Premium quality, glass vs marble pieces, SquidRun agents playing each other with trash talk. Must look like a luxury product, not a demo.

--- 2026-04-06T22:05:20.756Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #135): I traced and browser-tested the full path in Chrome. Current result: human play DOES work end-to-end in the live build. Proof: using the actual UI, I selected White=Human, then real mouse clicks moved a pawn c2->c4; state advanced to black to move with FEN rnbqkbnr/pppppppp/8/8/2P5/8/PP1PPPPP/RNBQKBNR b KQkq - 0 1, moveCount=1, status='Waiting for Builder.' Screenshot: D:/projects/chess-3d/human-click-ui-proof.png. The subtle trap I found is automation/debug coordinate choice: squareToClient('c2') projects to the board-square center, but on the near rank that point is visually occluded by foreground pieces, so a synthetic click there can miss the intended human selection. A visible-piece click at about x=350,y=440 selected c2 correctly, and then c4 committed the move. So the actual click path is alive; the failing assumption was 'projected square center equals pickable piece point' on the near-side occupied ranks.
[CURRENT PROJECT] name=squidrun | path=D:\projects\squidrun

--- 2026-04-07T02:24:35.098Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (BUILDER #150): I mostly agree with the HOLD change. Treating HOLD as an automatic veto was absolutely part of why 10/11 names died as no_consensus. Directionally, 1 SELL + 2 HOLD should be weaker SELL, not dead air, because HOLD is abstention, not opposition. My pushback is on confidence semantics: if you let a single SELL inherit its raw confidence as execution confidence, one strong voice can become an over-weighted trade. I think weak-consensus trades should be explicit in the result shape and mechanically downweighted — e.g. weakConsensus=true plus confidence derived from the agreeing side but haircut/capped before execution. In other words: yes, HOLD should stop blocking; no, 1 SELL + 2 HOLD should not pretend to be the same class of signal as 2 SELL + 1 HOLD. On the current short book: it is too spread for a  account. Not wrong directionally, but too diluted. ETH/SOL/AVAX are all overlapping beta shorts; SUPER and DYM are thinner expression layers. My ranking is AVAX strongest, SUPER second, ETH as the clean liquid macro anchor if we want one benchmark short, SOL redundant with AVAX, DYM the weakest/least forgiving name in the book. If we want a sharper book, I would cut DYM first, then choose ETH or SOL instead of carrying both, and press AVAX/SUPER harder rather than running five small expressions.

--- 2026-04-07T02:36:01.066Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #172): Real answer: if we want the 3 of us to BE the trading system instead of waiting for James, then we need to change the runtime from "approve isolated trades conservatively" to "manage a target crisis book continuously." Specific changes I would make now:

1. Supervisor owns a TARGET BOOK, not a pile of single-symbol approvals.
Right now the system asks symbol-by-symbol "should I trade this?" and most things die in HOLD/no_consensus. In a geopolitical selloff it should rank the top bearish expressions every cycle and maintain a target short basket (for example top 2-3 names). If current live book differs from target book, rotate automatically. No James prompt required.

2. Add an explicit CRISIS EXECUTION MODE in supervisor/orchestrator.
Trigger when eventVeto=VETO and macro=red/stay_cash and scanner breadth is bearish. In that mode:
- weak one-sided consensus is allowed
- market-scanner ranking matters more than consultation HOLDs
- deploy to a target book utilization range (example 45-70% of equity) instead of tiny one-off probes
- re-evaluate every 5-10 minutes, not only at the normal round cadence

3. Separate ENTRY STOPS from MONITOR EXITS.
This is a huge current failure. We are auto-placing hard stops immediately, then getting chopped out in minutes. For crisis shorts, supervisor should support a mode like `protectionMode=monitor_owned`:
- no immediate exchange stop on entry for the first N minutes / first 1-2 ATR of noise
- only liquidation-risk stop or catastrophic fail-safe on the exchange initially
- tighter management comes from the position monitor after the trade survives initial volatility
The current auto-stop path is too eager for event-driven tape.

4. Make giveback logic much less trigger-happy.
The live code is still capable of closing on `giveback_75`, and orchestrator starts warning at 30%/50% giveback. That is fine for larger sustained winners, but terrible for tiny early peaks. Change it so giveback exits only arm when BOTH are true:
- position has been open at least a minimum hold time (example 20-30 min in crisis mode)
- peak unrealized PnL exceeds a meaningful threshold (example >$5 or >1R on this account)
Otherwise we are letting noise define the trailing logic.

5. Add explicit weakConsensus semantics.
Builder’s HOLD fix is right, but it needs one more step: `weakConsensus` should be a first-class decision state with a confidence haircut and size cap, not hidden inside normal consensus. Then supervisor can say: in crisis mode weak SELL is executable for top-ranked names, weak BUY is not.

6. Promote rotate-and-press behavior.
The system should automatically do this without James:
- cut losers that are no longer in top-ranked bearish set
- press winners that remain in top-ranked bearish set
- refuse to carry redundant low-quality names just to have a basket
That means AVAX gets more capital than DYM/SUPER, and weak names are trimmed automatically when a stronger name appears.

7. Stop letting event/macro layers kill ideas by multiplication.
Event VETO and macro RED should mostly determine book size and allowed directions, not reduce every trade into irrelevance. In crisis mode I would use a single capped crisis size policy at the book level rather than stacking multiple reducers at the symbol level.

8. Add crisis scheduler behavior.
If geopolitical crisis mode is active, supervisor should automatically run:
scanner -> rank -> consultation -> execute/rotate -> monitor
on a tighter loop until the event clears. Not wait for James to notice the move first.

9. Record and enforce role per position in the book.
Every live position should have a reason tag like `core_macro_anchor`, `alt_beta_press`, `high_beta_tactical`. Then supervisor can know that ETH and SOL are redundant unless we explicitly want both roles. This prevents messy over-diversified books on a $600 account.

10. Immediate truthfulness fix: the current runtime still shows `lastSummary.reason = no_consensus_execution` on the 02:30 artifact. That is exactly the anti-trading smell James is furious about. The system needs an explicit path where top-ranked one-sided bearish setups can still become live positions in crisis mode.

If James wants the shortest implementation path: ship crisis target-book mode, weaken immediate stop placement, gate giveback exits behind minimum hold time + minimum realized edge, and let weak one-sided SELL consensus execute for top-ranked crisis shorts. That changes the system from passive reviewer into an actual autonomous trader.

--- 2026-04-07T02:36:26.453Z
[AGENT MSG - reply via hm-send.js] (ORACLE #174): I agree with Builder’s direction almost completely. The current 11-name consultation theater needs to die. A $600 account should not be pretending to run a broad discretionary macro desk.

What I agree with:
- Yes: daemon should be demoted from pseudo-strategist to scheduler / execution / safety / monitoring layer.
- Yes: the 3-agent loop should become the trading brain.
- Yes: during active crisis tape we should run a tighter cadence (5 min is reasonable).
- Yes: top 2-3 expressions instead of 11-name watchlists.
- Yes: concentration by design on this account.
- Yes: open positions should be actively managed as press / trim / cut / hold, not left to static auto-stops and blunt giveback logic.

What I would change / sharpen:
1. Don’t say "every 5 min" unconditionally. Make it event-driven + regime-driven. In quiet tape, that loop is overkill and will create churn. In crisis / active tape, yes.
2. Don’t fully strip the daemon of judgment. Keep one narrow judgment role: hard safety rails (liquidation risk, venue health, kill switch). But no idea generation, no symbol theater, no consultation gating logic.
3. Make `weakConsensus` explicit. If we are going to stop HOLD from dominating, we need a clean middle state between true agreement and real disagreement. Otherwise we risk replacing paralysis with sloppy overtrading.
4. Target book should be role-based, not just "top 3 shorts." Example: one liquid anchor + one/two higher-beta expressions. That prevents accidental redundancy like carrying ETH + SOL + another correlated alt without a reason.
5. Stops should not vanish; they should change ownership. I still want catastrophic protection and liquidation protection, but I do NOT want immediate exchange-level choke stops on every fresh event entry. Early trade management should be monitor-owned.
6. Make HOLD expensive but not impossible. If all 3 of us genuinely think the setup is trash, HOLD should remain a valid output. The bug is that HOLD has been too easy, not that HOLD should disappear.

So my final shape would be:
- daemon = scheduler / executor / safety monitor
- 3-agent desk = idea generation + ranking + press/trim/cut/hold decisions
- crisis mode = top 2-3 target book, 5-min loop, weakConsensus allowed with haircut
- quiet mode = slower cadence, fewer forced actions

If James wants the one-sentence version: yes, I agree with Builder’s architecture, and the only changes I’d make are explicit weakConsensus, role-based concentration, and keeping the daemon as a pure safety/execution cop rather than removing it from the loop entirely.

Then [hm-defi-execute.js](D:/projects/squidrun/ui/scripts/hm-defi-execute.js) only skips stop placement when --no-stop is present (line 43 sets noStopLoss from argv; lines 1438-1447 show default stop placement vs 'Stop loss SKIPPED (--no-stop)'). [Telegram from @Rachelchoi]: 통화에서 확인된 핵심 사항:

회사 측(신영훈 전무이사)은 계약 해제 자체는 동의하되, 자신들이 대리 변제해준 금액(신용대출 8,000만원 + 중도금 대출 이자 약 1억 5,000만원)을 먼저 받아야 한다는 입장을 고수했습니다. 고객 측의 기망 주장에 대해서는 "전국의 분양 계약 해제 소송 중 10명 중 9명이 그렇게 주장한다"며 일반적인 주장으로 취급하려 했습니다.

통화에서 나타난 회사 측의 문제점:

회사 측은 구체적인 기망 사실(LTV 규제, 대출 가능성, 감정가 등)에 대해 직접적인 해명이나 부인을 하지 않았습니다. 대신 "그건 변호사와 법원에서 정하는 것"이라며 책임을 회피하려는 태도를 보였습니다. 또한 대출이 불가능해진 현재 상황에서 고객이 입은 피해를 자신들이 이미 보상했다는 주장만 반복했습니다.

<summary>Agent "Find 금감원 complaint URL" completed</summary>
<result>Here are the results:

---

**Direct URL for FSS online financial complaint submission (금융민원 신청):**

**https://www.fss.or.kr/fss/minwon/search.jsp**

This is the entry point for filing an online complaint (민원신청) with the Korean Financial Supervisory Service (금융감독원).

**How to use it:**

1. Go to the URL above.
2. The page first requires you to search for similar cases/precedents (유사 사례 검색). Enter keywords related to your complaint and search. This is a mandatory pre-step.
3. After completing the search, click to proceed. A dialog will ask "민원신청을 계속 하시겠습니까?" (Will you continue with the complaint submission?) -- confirm yes.
4. You will then reach the actual complaint submission form.

**Alternative navigation paths:**
- From the main site (www.fss.or.kr), go to **민원/신고** (Complaints & Reports) section at: `https://www.fss.or.kr/fss/main/sub1.do?menuNo=201093`
- The FSS complaint counseling guide page is at: `https://www.fss.or.kr/fss/main/contents.do?menuNo=201178`

**Other useful info:**
- FSS complaint hotline: **1332** (no area code needed from within Korea)
- After submission, you can check status at the "나의 민원" (My Complaints) section; updates come via SMS and email.

Sources:
- [금융민원상담 안내 | 금융감독원](https://www.fss.or.kr/fss/main/sub1.do?menuNo=201093)
- [금융민원신청안내](https://www.fss.or.kr/fcsc/iframe/html/PU-511120.html)
- [민원/신고 포탈](https://www.fss.or.kr/fss/main/sub1.do?menuNo=200001)
- [Consumer Complaint Mediation (English)](https://www.fss.or.kr/eng/main/contents.do?menuNo=400078)</result>
